review_text: Cyberpunk 2077 is more than just a video game; it is a deep dive into a fascinating and complex world. Playing this game feels like stepping into a living, breathing city where every street has a history. Night City is built with incredible detail, and the game does an amazing job of showing how advanced technology changes everyday life. One of the best parts of the game is the rich lore and the detailed in-game technology. The game explores how the physical world connects with advanced digital systems, which makes the setting feel very grounded and realistic. Learning about how the cyberware works and mapping out the history of the different corporations is incredibly engaging. The game puts a lot of effort into explaining the technical side of its universe, making it a joy to explore for anyone who appreciates well-thought-out digital and physical networks.

review_text: You said "ala akhbar" and I changed it to "allahu akbar" which isn't what you wrote. I'll keep your original phrasing next time. And you're right, I dropped the part about not finishing it. Here's the corrected version: Baldur's Gate 3 is one of the better arguments for the CRPG genre reaching a mainstream audience it hasn't touched since the late 90s. Larian built something with a production quality and level of reactivity that made it impossible to ignore even for people who had never touched Divinity or the original Baldur's Gate games, and the results speak for themselves in terms of how many people it pulled into a style of game they would never have otherwise tried. The storytelling is the strongest part. The writing across the main cast is consistently good and the way the game responds to your choices with actual consequence rather than the illusion of consequence is what separates it from most RPGs claiming to offer the same thing. The companions have enough depth that spending time with them feels worthwhile rather than obligatory, which for a game asking this many hours of you matters more than people give it credit for. The systemic flexibility is where it earns its reputation most honestly. Talking your way through a fight, finding an environmental solution nobody intended, or handing Gale the nuclear option and ala akhbar-ing your way to the credits (which was my exit) instead of finishing the game properly, the game accommodates all of it without blinking. That openness is rare and Larian spent years refining it across their previous work before landing here. The overhype conversation is fair though. It is an exceptional game and also a very long one that asks real patience before it fully opens up, and the discourse around launch inflated expectations in ways the game itself was never going to satisfy for everyone. Worth going back and actually finishing this time. Works well on both PC and Steam Deck.

title: IdleOn
game_description: The management RPG where your heroes keep fighting even when you're gone! Spend their loot on upgrades, unlock classes from Elemental Wizard to Cannon Pirate, and level up 19 unique skills like Farming, Sailing, and even Construction!
review_text: Very fun incremental game with a lot ever-expanding content to get through. Yes you can swipe to progress faster, but progression feels good F2P and I've never felt the need to spend in order to have fun. The "skill" if you want to call it that is in understanding which of the game's systems to focus on at a time. There are dozens of interlocking mechanics and your 10 characters can each engage with almost all of them, so the gameplay is mostly about deciding who's fighting monsters, who's paying tribute to the Monument of Justice, who's slumming it in an early game zone for rare drops, etc. This game also has a LOT of community support. People have built all sorts of calculators, guides, and other resources for optimizing the game. There's just a lot to sink your teeth into here.

review_text: I know my hours for some people wanting to get into this looks like alot, but I promise I'm not even that deep into it. review_text: Early Access Review I"m a HoMM5 ride or die. Have been since I was a young child playing on my dad's home computer. This one feels like it's taking elements from 5 and 3, like a lot from 3, and building on what worked in those games. I haven't even played 3 but I know it's very well liked among the fans. This game has been smooth as silk to play. It's intuitive for series veterans. It has a great visual design. The music is decent. I, personally, prefer the music and general art direction of 5 with it's loud and proud philosophy. The cinematic fly-by for each town in that game will never not be my favorite way to do it. This game doesn't quite hit those artistic highs in my view, but the gameplay is every bit as on brand. Spells are impactful, creatures do heinous war crimes as part of their regular ability sets, and items boost your already ridiculous arsenal up that much higher. Welcome back, Heroes of Might and Magic. Please, for the love of all that is good in this gaming landscape, don't be a stranger.

review_text: Early Access Review Most people consider HoMM 3 the gold standard of the series. While I enjoyed it, HoMM 2 was the game that made me fall in love with the franchise at first sight. The graphics, the epic music, and even the genre itself, which I hadn’t really explored before, all clicked with me. For context, I’m not a huge strategy fan. RTS is actually one of my least favorite genres. With that in mind, I’ve been following the news around Olden Era closely and was curious to see where it would go. Previous entries tried to capture that old-school magic with mixed results, but Olden Era genuinely feels like a return to form. After about 14 hours, including a few campaign missions and the tutorials, I can confidently say this is a great HoMM installment. It even gave me flashes of that old feeling again without being a simple copy. I especially like the town and army upgrades, the focus system, the build synergies, and the ability to upgrade spells. It’s still in early access and a few issues can be found here and there, which is expected at this stage. That said, there’s plenty of room to grow, which is actually encouraging. There is a strong foundation here, and I expect it to get even better as more content is added and bugs are ironed out. TL;DR: Feels like classic HoMM again, but modernized and fun, with a few rough edges. Definitely recommended.

review_text: Early Access Review Игра, возможно, ещё не завершена, но уже очень хороша. Видна любовь к игре со стороны разработчиков, за что им большой респект. Плюсы: арт, атмосфера, баланс — всё на месте. Очень-очень крепкая база для игры, думаю, её ждёт большое будущее и огромная поддержка сообщества. Минусы: пока есть немного багов и маловато «мяса» по количеству контента еще не доходит до тройки. Если более конкретно, то сильно не хватает кораблей и подземелий, ИИ какой-то злой, прям не расслабишься с ним. Мой совет тем, кто жалуется на графику и плохую видимость на карте приключений: включите в настройках подсветку интерактивных объектов на «всегда», потом, как привыкнете, выключите. Ещё один совет: отключите пролёт камеры. Оно, конечно, красиво, но часа через два начинает бесить. Ещё один ход — и спать... Голосом Каневского: «Спать, конечно же, никто не пошёл».

review_text: This is literally the future of MMO gaming, for better or worse. And since it's free, if you're an enthusiast of Exploration/MMO/Open world genres, I recommend to try it. It's far from a perfect game, mind you - but it does try a lot of experimental things, and gets a noticeable number of them right... or almost right. See below. Some outstanding features: [+++] Customizable experience (controls, difficulty, UI, social features) |+++] Ridiculous QoL (tooltips tell how to acquire/use all items, filters on map to see enemy or resource spawns, equipment filtering, auto disassembly, etc) [ + ] Simple ARPG combat that is actually 80% optional [???] AI chatbot NPCs to up relations. It's barebones and only funny 5 minutes. [ + ] AI Voice/Appearance import from external media in char creation [ + ] Assisted blocking with slowmo - Optional [ - ] Mobile slop monetization, mostly cosmetics [+++] Heavily exploration centric game with vibes to match [+++] I'm not kidding, you can walk in on bandit outposts and play chess with them [---] Menu hell, you spend half of the game in menus unlocking things [????????????????] Social feature overload: public gifting, chat transcript, voice chat, in-chat voice chat, you can literally level up your relation with other players, partnerships/disciples system, on-map signs for other players, pre-made raidgroups, guilds (yes, separate), bounty system -- there's more than i could even imagine [+++] 2 profession (scholar/healer) both for exploration and group content, also as "mercenary" to help other players (cure them, clear their name, etc) [???] PvP invade and kill people that did in fact not clear their name [--] Bounty systems for the above stuff has insane player competition, i could never find a job for it [...] Ramping up flashy main story (feels like Warframe, negative for me but...) [ + ] Mobile style boss assistance by other players and Coop mode assistance [ + ] Actual full-on online MMO mode, although limited [???] There's literally a Voice Changer option in Voice Chat Overall this feels like some kind of Ubisoft MMO prototype from 2032, after they came back from the brink of death and made bank on AI and Mobile gaming. This comes with absolutely ALL the bell and whistles associated, both good and bad. I can't guarantee you'll like it, especially since in some regards it's really not that good, but it's still largely viable. If anything, I recommend to try it out just to get a feel of all the odd takes and experiments in it.

review_text: Before the Lord of Hatred update i would have given this game a thumbs up. I've had my fun playing. I got to be creative in creating my own builds. The progress is clear and if you were willing and had the time on your hands you could strive to get to the end of the endgame and have fun while doing so. That said, after they released this last dlc it all went downhill for me. - They said they are going to include lootfilter in the game - it's in dlc only - so behind a paywall - They made changes to EVERY CLASS already in the game, changing how their respective talent tree looks and works - THEY BLOCKED CERTAIN SKILL VARIANTS FOR EACH AND EVERY CLASS AFTER THIS DLC - meaning in the base game, before the update you had everything they had made, given to you - now, because they released the dlc, they took it from you, forcing you to buy the new content. Essentially they want you to pay again for part of the game you already had payed for. That's a big no no for me. - Making a new season - season 13, but not including ANY content along with it. They made it as a courtesy, for the purpose of saying 'buy our dlc'. - I was hesitant on buying this dlc, wanted to wait for the reaction/to see how it was received. When it dropped i had to download a whole 60GB of nothing. I knew of the greed of this company but this is the first time i got to experience it first hand. All this, is why i dont recommend it.

review_text: I can only agree with all other comments to the Lord of Hatred Update. Im not a full-time Diablo Player but I still enjoyed playing a few characters over some seasons to about 200 Paragon Levels. With the first expansion, new classes, areas and campaign quests were added. If you didnt buy it, you couldnt play that - absolutely fair and a normal DLC. With this expansion, classes, areas and campaign quests and crucially a skill tree rework were added. Which is fair, apart from the fact that it locks skills from classes YOU ALREADY PAID FOR. Originally I wanted to play the warlock and was only held back by the 40€ price tag on a DLC, but okay, i can still play a sorcerer or spiritborn right? Well, only partially as skills you need for pretty much every good build are just locked behind a DLC for a different character... IDK, i've played so many games over the last few years but this feels like one of the worst things I've witnessed, I never had parts of content I already paid for locked later. If it gets changed its a good DLC and the game is fun but holy did this kill all motivation for this game for me.

title: Firestone – Idle Clicker Online RPG
game_description: Chill out, your heroes got this! They’ll fight even when you’re offline. Pop back in for satisfying numbers going up, sweet rewards and easy level-ups. Unlock cool gear, & smack down epic bosses. Play idle or clicker - you’ll power up with no stress, just good times and infinite progression!
review_text: I’ve put close to 1000 hours into Firestone – Idle Clicker Online RPG, and honestly, that’s the only reason I feel qualified to say this: I can’t recommend it. At its core, the game is actually fun. The idle progression, hero collection, and steady upgrades are satisfying, and it’s easy to see why it hooks people. It’s designed to be a chill, AFK-style experience where your team progresses even when you’re offline . But the deeper you go, the more the monetization completely undermines everything good about it. This game is massively pay-to-win. You can technically play for free, but reaching anything close to the top without spending money is basically impossible. Progress slows to a crawl, and you’re constantly hit with FOMO-driven offers, limited-time deals, and “special” bundles clearly designed to push you into spending. It stops feeling like a game and starts feeling like a storefront. Another major issue is how the servers are handled. New servers keep popping up, drawing in fresh players, while older servers stagnate and eventually get merged together. Meanwhile, the real activity and competition stay on the newest servers, making long-term investment feel pointless. So yeah—fun gameplay loop, but buried under predatory P2W systems, FOMO tactics, and questionable server management. Even after 1000 hours, I can’t recommend it.

review_text: It took me a few patches, but this game genuinely changed how I approach life. Version 1.0 didn't impress me much. The optimization issues were rough, and the story felt lackluster. But something told me to keep going - and I'm really glad I did. Everything changed with 1.1. Jinshi and Jue were the first moment the game truly opened my eyes. Then Shorekeeper arrived in 1.3, and that was it - I was hooked. It's not that the story suddenly became some untouchable masterpiece (though Kuro genuinely raises the bar with every patch). It's the soul behind it. You can feel how much care goes into every update, and watching the devs grow and improve patch after patch is honestly inspiring - it's become one of the things that motivates me to push harder in my own life. The lore expansions keep getting better, constantly revealing cool new sides of this world. Visually, Rinascita in 2.0 is stunning, and the new area in 3.3 is on another level - you can feel the Elden Ring inspiration, and I'm absolutely here for it. My only real complaint is the heavy NPC culling in Lahai-Roi, but the devs are clearly aware and actively working on it. I'll just say this: the lore and the vibe of this game have genuinely made me a better person. That's not something I say about many games.

review_text: Wuthering Waves is the perfect example of a game that looks incredible on the surface but immediately falls apart the moment you actually try to play it. It’s like the developers built a gorgeous sports car and then forgot to install an engine. Or wheels. Or anything that makes it move. The combat feels amazing for about five minutes, right up until you realize half the enemies are allergic to being fun. Everything either evaporates instantly or turns into a brick wall that drags the fight out long enough for you to reconsider your life choices. And the balance? It swings around so wildly you’d think they’re tuning the game by rolling dice. Every patch feels like the devs are trying to speedrun how fast they can turn a promising combat system into a chore. The story is a masterpiece of confusion. It’s like reading a novel where every third page is missing and the remaining pages are out of order. The game keeps acting like you should be emotionally invested in characters you met five minutes ago and lore that’s explained with the clarity of a fogged‑up bathroom mirror. And then there’s the performance. The game stutters so hard you’d think your device is trying to escape. Cutscenes lag, menus lag, sometimes even the loading screens lag. It’s almost impressive how consistently inconsistent it is.
